lesson.menu_desc: Self-care vocabulary, perfect modals & a reading on resilience -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 5 -- Wellbeing & Resilience
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 5 -- Wellbeing & Resilience

paras: For most of her career, Marina treated rest as a reward she had to earn. She answered emails at midnight, skipped lunch to finish reports, and wore her exhaustion like a badge of honor. When colleagues praised her stamina she felt proud; when her body finally protested with months of insomnia and a creeping sense of dread, she was genuinely surprised. Looking back, she admits she should have listened to the early warnings instead of pushing through them.

Recovery did not come from a single dramatic change. It came from small, sustainable habits: a real lunch break, a walk without her phone, a firm boundary around her evenings. None of it felt heroic, and at first it even felt a little lazy. Yet within a few months she noticed she could bounce back from a hard day far more quickly than before, and the dread had quietly lifted.

Now Marina tells younger colleagues the advice she wishes someone had given her: you ought to protect your downtime as fiercely as you protect a deadline. Resilience, she has learned, is not about never falling down. It is about building a life steady enough that, when you do fall, you have somewhere soft to land.
reading.source: Adapted from a wellbeing feature, The Quiet Hours

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about Marina's recovery?
choices: She recovered by making one big, dramatic change to her job.
Small, sustainable habits and firm boundaries slowly rebuilt her resilience.
She learned that rest must always be earned through hard work first.

vocabnote.text: Notice how the writer talks about the past: "she should have listened" and "the advice she wishes someone had given her." Both look back and gently criticize a past choice. That is exactly what perfect modals do.

modals.title: Looking back: perfect modals of advice
cards: A good idea you did NOT do. "I should have set boundaries earlier."
Something you did but now regret. "I shouldn't have skipped so many lunches."
A near-synonym of should have. "She ought to have listened to the warnings."
Urgent advice for the present, NOT the past. "You'd better slow down before you burn out."

items: Early in her career, Marina saw rest as something she had to earn.
t
She treated rest as a reward she had to earn.
Marina recovered through one single dramatic change.
f
Recovery came from small, sustainable habits, not one big change.
At first, the new habits felt heroic and exciting to her.
f
At first they felt a little lazy, not heroic.
She now advises younger colleagues to guard their downtime.
t
She tells them to protect their downtime as fiercely as a deadline.

rephrase.title: Rewrite each idea with a perfect modal
items: It was a mistake to answer emails at midnight.
shouldn't have
Resting earlier would have been a good idea for her.
should have
Listening to the warning signs was the wise thing to do.
ought to have
My advice now: stop and recharge before you collapse.
had better
Taking that downtime would have helped me bounce back.
should have

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: She shouldn't have answered emails at midnight.
She ought to have listened to the warning signs.
You'd better stop and recharge before you collapse.
I should have taken that downtime to bounce back.
answerkey.note: Form check: should / shouldn't / ought to + have + past participle for the past; had better + base verb for an urgent warning about now.

listenings.text: Honestly, last year nearly broke me. I kept up with everyone else, said yes to everything, and never once slowed down. By December I was completely overwhelmed and heading straight for burnout. Looking back, I should have set boundaries far sooner, and I ought to have protected my downtime instead of giving it away. What saved me was almost embarrassingly simple. I started to unwind in the evenings, I built a few sustainable habits, and I let myself recharge on the weekends. It took time, but I bounced back. If you are running on empty right now, take it from me: you had better slow down before your body forces you to.

People assume retirement is the moment you finally relax, but I have learned that resilience is a habit you build long before you stop working. For years I treated self-care as a reward I had to earn, something for after the work was done. That was a mistake. These days I see work-life balance differently. I protect my downtime the way I protect an important meeting. I unwind without guilt, I recharge before I am empty, not after. A slower pace has not made me less capable. If anything, it has made me steadier, calmer, and far better at bouncing back when life gets hard.
